title: CEMAC Act
plain_summary: This bill stops the US from supporting International Monetary Fund actions with Central African countries. The US will block this support until the IMF makes a clear rule that money oil companies set aside for environmental cleanup cannot be counted as foreign currency reserves. The bill was created because Central African banking rules are forcing oil companies to put cleanup money in special accounts, which is hurting American businesses that invest in that area. The US wants to protect American oil company investments by making sure cleanup funds are treated differently from regular reserves.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Financial Services.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Central African Exploitation and Manipulation of American Companies Act or the CEMAC Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Findings
sections.text: Congress has found the following facts. The member countries of the Central African Economic Monetary Community (CEMAC) have large amounts of oil and gas underground. International oil companies have worked in these countries for many decades and invested money there. In two thousand eighteen, the central bank for CEMAC called the Bank of Central African States introduced a new rule. This rule would force oil companies to send money to the bank. This money is supposed to be used later to clean up and restore land after oil drilling is finished. Over the last seven years, the oil companies and the bank have tried to work out problems with this rule. They have fixed twenty three issues, but big problems still remain. The bank refuses to give up legal protections that make it hard to sue them. The bank also wants to control the cleanup money accounts. The rule also has unfair punishment terms. The bank set a deadline of April thirtieth, two thousand twenty five for oil companies to agree to this rule. If companies do not agree by then, they will have to pay penalties equal to one hundred fifty percent of their cleanup funds starting May first, two thousand twenty five. This new rule will hurt oil and gas investment in Central Africa for a long time. It will make an already difficult place to invest much worse. The CEMAC member countries have said these cleanup funds will help increase their foreign money reserves. But cleanup funds are only meant to pay for cleaning up drilling sites. The International Monetary Fund says these funds do not meet the requirements to count as foreign money reserves. The International Monetary Fund rules say money must be easily available and controlled by a country's money authorities to count as foreign reserves. Oil and gas investments in the CEMAC region have been going down since two thousand eighteen. This new bank rule will make the decline much faster. Standard and Poor's estimates that by two thousand fifty, this rule will reduce government income for CEMAC member countries by eighty six billion dollars. It will also reduce business investment in the region by forty five billion dollars. The International Monetary Fund has refused to clearly state that these cleanup funds will not count as foreign money reserves. This has misled the CEMAC member countries and put tens of billions of dollars of oil company investments at risk.

sections.title: Section 3  Statement of Policy
sections.text: The United States believes the following things. Having United States companies invest in other regions is good for those areas. Any money that oil companies give to the Bank of Central African States for cleaning up drilling sites cannot count toward the foreign money reserves of CEMAC member countries. This is based on the rules in the International Monetary Fund's Balance of Payments and International Investment Position Manual. The International Monetary Fund must clearly tell countries what money can and cannot count as foreign reserves. This helps countries make good financial decisions. If the rule forcing oil companies to send cleanup money to the Bank of Central African States becomes law, the International Monetary Fund would be responsible for the loss of investment in the CEMAC region.

sections.title: Section 4  Withdrawal of Funding
sections.text: The Secretary of the Treasury must work with the United States Executive Director at the International Monetary Fund and the Secretary of State to make an important decision. Until they make this decision, the following things will happen. The President and no other person or agency can vote to approve any International Monetary Fund action related to any CEMAC member country on behalf of the United States. The Secretary of the Treasury must tell the United States Executive Director at the International Monetary Fund to use America's voice and vote to oppose certain proposals. These include any proposal to increase a CEMAC member country's share in the International Monetary Fund or to change the exceptional access policy of the International Monetary Fund for any CEMAC member country. The decision that must be made is this. The International Monetary Fund must publicly state clearly that any money given to the Bank of Central African States by any international oil company for cleaning up drilling sites cannot count toward any country's foreign money reserves. Once the Secretary of the Treasury makes this decision, they must make it public and send a copy to the appropriate congressional committees. The Secretary of the Treasury must provide a report to the appropriate congressional committees no later than thirty days after making the decision. If the decision has not been made within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed, the Secretary must still provide the report by then. The report must explain what actions the United States Government has taken at the International Monetary Fund to get them to publicly state that cleanup money from oil companies to the Bank of Central African States cannot count as foreign money reserves. The appropriate congressional committees are the Committee on Financial Services and the Committee on Foreign Affairs in the House of Representatives, and the Committee on Finance and the Committee on Foreign Relations in the Senate.
